title: Martin Amis: An Appreciation
summary: Our critic assesses the achievement of Martin Amis, Britain’s most famous literary son.

title: Book Review: ‘NB by J.C.,’ by James Campbell
summary: “NB by J.C.” collects the variegated musings of James Campbell in the Times Literary Supplement.

title: In ‘Fires in the Dark,’ Kay Redfield Jamison Turns to Healers
summary: In “Fires in the Dark,” Jamison, known for her expertise on manic depression, delves into the quest to heal. Her new book, she says, is a “love song to psychotherapy.”

title: The Detective Novel ‘Whose Body?,’ by Dorothy L. Sayers, Turns 100
summary: Dorothy L. Sayers dealt with emotional and financial instability by writing “Whose Body?,” the first of many to star the detective Lord Peter Wimsey.

title: Book Review: ‘Dom Casmurro,’ by Machado de Assis
summary: “Dom Casmurro,” by Machado de Assis, teaches us to read — and reread — with precise detail and masterly obfuscation.

title: Book Review: ‘The Late Americans,’ by Brandon Taylor
summary: Brandon Taylor’s novel circulates among Iowa City residents, some privileged, some not, but all aware that their possibilities are contracting.

title: Martin Amis’s Best Books: A Guide
summary: The acclaimed British novelist was also an essayist, memoirist and critic of the first rank.

title: The Best Romance Novels of 2024 (So Far)
summary: Looking for an escapist love story? Here are 2024’s sexiest, swooniest reads.

title: What Book Should You Read Next?
summary: Finding a book you’ll love can be daunting. Let us help.

title: Koji Suzuki, Sometimes Called the Stephen King of Japan, Dies at 68
summary: His “Ring” trilogy helped create a genre known as J-horror and spawned a multimedia franchise, including one of the highest-grossing horror films ever made.

title: Are These the Bones of the Fourth Musketeer? This Dutch Village Hopes So.
summary: Wolder, near the Belgian border, is waiting to see if the skeleton it dug up in a church is Count d’Artagnan, from Alexandre Dumas’s tale.

title: Closed-Door Romance Books That Will Make You Swoon
summary: The best-selling author Lynn Painter recommends low-heat love stories that deliver on juicy tension, crackling banter and plenty of swoon.

title: Amor Towles Shares Insights on His Own Rare Book Collection
summary: The best-selling author and collector explains what draws him to add to his own bookshelves.

title: Revolution is the Theme at the Firsts London Book Fair
summary: What to expect at the book fair in the Saatchi Gallery.

title: How to See Rare Books in London
summary: Here’s a guide to illuminated manuscripts, antique tomes and first editions around the British capital.

title: Book Review: ‘Seek the Traitor’s Son,’ by Veronica Roth
summary: In Veronica Roth’s “Seek the Traitor’s Son,” a soldier in a pandemic-ravaged world is forced to become the hope of her people.

title: Book Review: ‘AI for Good,’ by Josh Tyrangiel
summary: In “AI for Good,” Josh Tyrangiel travels to the classrooms, hospitals and research labs where people are using artificial intelligence that might benefit society.

title: Book Review: ‘Nerve Damage,’ by Annakeara Stinson
summary: “Nerve Damage,” by Annakeara Stinson, is a jittery psychological thriller about a woman whose creepy ex simply won’t leave her alone.

title: Book Review: ‘Men Like Ours,’ by Bindu Bansinath
summary: “Men Like Ours,” a novel by Bindu Bansinath, follows an immigrant family through a community crisis.

title: Amitav Ghosh Brings the Main Character of ‘Ghost Eye’ to Life, With the Help of a Sketch Artist
summary: With the help of a forensic artist, Amitav Ghosh puts a face to the name of Varsha Gupta, the central figure of his new novel, “Ghost Eye.”

title: Book Review: ‘When the Forest Breathes,’ by Suzanne Simard
summary: Suzanne Simard’s new book urges Western science to take a lesson from the more holistic Indigenous approach to forest preservation.

title: New Books Provide Divergent Views of the Art Market
summary: Three new books — a sweeping work of nonfiction, a cheeky memoir and a dual biography — provide divergent views on the business of buying and selling, and they are out just in time for New York Art Week.

title: Book Review: ‘Look What You Made Me Do,’ by John Lanchester
summary: In John Lanchester’s “Look What You Made Me Do,” a widow is unnerved when a hit TV series airs details from her marriage a little too closely.

title: Poetry Review: ‘Wellwater,’ by Karen Solie
summary: The Canadian poet Karen Solie balances environmental concerns with hope and deadpan wit.

title: A Traveling Bookstore Keeps on Rolling
summary: Rita Collins had a dream for her retirement: bringing books and people together all over the country. Behind the wheel of a van she’s making it happen.

title: Book Review: ‘Selling Opportunity,’ by Mary Lisa Gavenas
summary: In “Selling Opportunity,” Mary Lisa Gavenas tells the not-always-rosy story of Mary Kay, the brand — and its founder.

title: The Best Books to Read on American Empire
summary: As these books show, the United States has long struggled to reconcile its imperial ambition with its founding ideals, prompting detractors at home and abroad.

title: Edith Eva Eger, Psychologist Who Barely Survived Auschwitz, Dies at 98
summary: Her time in concentration camps brought her an understanding of humanity that helped her treat her patients.

title: J.H. Prynne, Erudite and Elusive British Poet, Dies at 89
summary: Even admirers admitted his densely intellectual work could be “punishing.” Still, some considered him one of England’s most important poets.

title: Xia De-hong, 94, Dies; Persecuted in China, She Starred in Daughter’s Memoir
summary: “Wild Swans,” a best-selling 1991 memoir, told the story of a stoic mother holding her family together amid torture and imprisonment under Mao’s regime.
